title: Acne Vulgaris & Rosacea
oneLiner: Two common chronic inflammatory facial dermatoses: acne (adolescents, closed/open comedones, inflamed nodules) vs rosacea (adults >30, central flushing, telangiectasias, papules/pustules but no comedones). Both disfiguring and treatable — misdiagnosis worsens outcomes.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic Acne Vignette:**  
A 17-year-old male presents with a 6-month history of spots on his face, upper chest, and back. He has both blackheads and whiteheads, plus red, tender papules and pustules. Some deeper, larger lumps under the skin are painful. He has tried over-the-counter benzoyl peroxide washes and salicylic acid. He feels embarrassed, avoids social situations, and picks at the lesions. There is no history of flushing or eye discomfort.

- **Teenager** → hormonal surge, sebum overproduction  
- **Face + upper trunk** → distribution of pilosebaceous units (sebaceous glands are largest here)  
- **Mixed lesions: comedones + inflammatory** → classic acne vulgaris; comedones are pathognomonic  
- **Painful deep nodules** → indicates inflammatory papules and cysts (risk of scarring)  
- **Picking** → can lead to secondary infection, hyperpigmentation, scars  
- **No flushing or ocular symptoms** → rules against rosacea  

**Classic Rosacea Vignette:**  
A 45-year-old woman with fair skin presents with persistent redness on her cheeks, nose, and central forehead. She reports episodes of flushing triggered by hot drinks, spicy food, and sun exposure. She has small red bumps and pus-filled spots on the same areas, but no blackheads or whiteheads. She also describes gritty, dry eyes and occasional blurring.

- **Middle-aged, fair-skinned, female** → typical demographics (rosacea women > men, but men more severe if later)  
- **Central face: cheeks, nose, forehead** → erythema and telangiectasias; spares perioral and periocular zones  
- **Flushing + triggers** → vascular dysregulation hallmark  
- **No comedones** → this is the single best differentiating feature from acne  
- **Papules and pustules on erythematous background** → “rose” color  
- **Ocular symptoms: gritty, dry eyes** → ocular rosacea (40-60% of patients, often missed)  

**Atypical Presentations That Can Trick You:**

|**Trickster Variant**|**Presentation**|**Why It’s a Trap**|
|---|---|---|
|Adult-onset acne (20s-30s) | Woman 28 with chin/jawline inflammatory cysts, sometimes with oily skin, mild hirsutism | Might be mistaken for rosacea because of age and facial redness; look for comedones and menstrual flare to confirm acne |
|Gram-negative folliculitis| Patient with long-term oral antibiotics for acne gets sudden worsening of perinasal pustules | Misinterpreted as treatment failure → could actually be iatrogenic (add swab+) |
|Rosacea fulminans (pyoderma faciale)| Sudden explosive pustular eruption on face in young women | Looks severe like cystic acne but no comedones; often confused with acne fulminans (acne fulminans has systemic symptoms) |

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Acne Vulgaris:** A chronic inflammatory disease of the pilosebaceous unit driven by four interlinked processes: follicular hyperkeratinisation leading to microcomedones, increased sebum production (androgens), colonisation by *Cutibacterium acnes*, and inflammation. It is the most common skin disease worldwide, affecting nearly 85% of adolescents and young adults.

**Rosacea:** A chronic cutaneous disorder of the central face characterised by flushing, persistent erythema, telangiectasias, and inflammatory papules/pustules. It is not a disease of the sebaceous follicle per se — there is no hyperkeratosis or excessive sebum — but rather a neurovascular dysregulation with aberrant innate immune responses. Rosacea almost always spares the perioral and periocular zones and is seen in adults, peaking in the 40-60 age group.

**Why They Matter Clinically:**

- **Frequency:** Acne is ubiquitous — you will see it daily in primary care, paediatrics, dermatology, and even emergency (for things like hydradenitis suppurativa dx). Rosacea affects up to 10% of adults.
- **Psychosocial Impact:** Both cause significant depression, anxiety, social withdrawal, and reduced QoL. Acne scarring is permanent.
- **Common Pitfalls:** Mistaking rosacea for acne leads to inappropriate topical retinoids/antibiotics that worsen flushing; mistaking acne for rosacea can deny a patient isotretinoin when indicated. Ocular rosacea is frequently not asked about, leading to corneal damage.
- **Medical Consequences:** Acne scarring is permanent; rosacea can cause keratitis and vision loss; both can be associated with systemic diseases (acne with PCOS/androgen excess; rosacea with metabolic syndrome? — evidence mixed but plausible).

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
**Acne — The Story in 4 Acts:**

**Act 1: Follicular hyperkeratosis.**  
Androgens stimulate both sebaceous gland size and activity. Inside the follicle, keratinocytes fail to desquamate normally, causing a plug — the **microcomedone**. This is the precursor of all acne lesions.  
*Why you see comedones:* The follicular opening becomes blocked. If open → blackhead (melanin keratin debris oxidises); if closed → whitehead.

**Act 2: Sebum overproduction.**  
Androgens (especially dihydrotestosterone) drive sebocytes to produce sebum rich in triglycerides and squalene. The follicle becomes distended.  
*Why you see oily skin:* Sebum excretion rate is increased.

**Act 3: *C. acnes* colonisation.**  
This commensal anaerobe thrives in the lipid-rich, oxygen-poor environment of the blocked follicle. It releases lipases, proteases, and hyaluronidases.  
*Why inflammation starts:* *C. acnes* activates toll-like receptor 2 (TLR2) on keratinocytes and macrophages, triggering IL-1, TNF-α, and complement. The follicle wall ruptures.

**Act 4: Inflammation and scarring.**  
Rupture of the follicular contents (keratin, sebum, *C. acnes*) into the dermis triggers a neutrophilic and lymphocytic response. This gives **inflammatory papules, pustules, nodules, and cysts**.  
*Why scars form:* The dermis is damaged. Collagenolytic activity and aberrant healing produce atrophic or hypertrophic scars.

**Rosacea — The Story of an Overreactive Innate System**

**Core abnormality:** An exaggerated innate immune response with neurovascular instability, not primarily a follicular problem.

**Trigger → Flushing:**  
Emotional stress, heat, UV, alcohol, and spicy food cause vasodilation via neuropeptide release (substance P, CGRP) from sensory nerves.  
*Why you see flushing and persistent erythema:* Repeated vasodilation over years leads to telangiectasias (dilated superficial capillaries) and chronic edema from leaky vessels.

**Inflammatory papules and pustules:**  
In rosacea, keratinocytes express high levels of cathelicidin (LL-37) and kallikrein 5. This antimicrobial peptide, when abnormally cleaved, becomes pro-inflammatory. The inflammation is perifollicular, but comedones are *absent* because there is no hyperkeratosis.  
*Why no comedones:* The follicular opening remains patent; the inflammation is in the surrounding dermis, not the follicle neck.

**Demodex mites:** The commensal *Demodex folliculorum* is found in higher densities in rosacea patients. They may act as a trigger by activating TLRs and producing bacterial products (*Bacillus oleronius*).  
*Why you see papules and pustules on a red background:* Mite-induced inflammation adds to the innate immune response.

**Ocular rosacea:** Melbomian gland dysfunction with tear film instability leads to blepharitis, conjunctival hyperaemia, and corneal damage.  
*Why gritty eyes:* Meibum quality changes → dry eye with inflammation.

**Connection to Pattern Recognition:**

- The **absence of comedones** in rosacea is the single most reliable clue to differentiate it from acne — histologically there is no follicular hyperkeratosis.
- The **central face distribution** with sparing of perioral area in rosacea vs. the full-face-plus-trunk of acne reflects different anatomical predilection: rosacea affects the “flush area” (triggered by vascular units), while acne hits sebaceous units anywhere.
- **Flushing** is pathognomonic for rosacea and never part of acne.

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins):
| **Category** | **Acne Vulgaris** | **Rosacea** |
|--------------|-------------------|-------------|
| **Idiopathic** | Multifactorial; genetic predisposition (twin studies: 50–80% heritability) | Genetic: familial clustering, HLA antigens; specific genes unknown |
| **Hormonal** | Androgens (adrenarche, puberty, PCOS, anabolic steroids, pregnancy). *Stem clue: “patient with irregular periods, hirsutism, obesity”* | Not primarily hormonal; but perimenopausal women may flare. *Stem clue: “midlife woman with hot flushes and facial redness”* |
| **Environmental / Lifestyle** | Diet: high glycemic index, dairy (skim milk? controversial). *Clue: “works in fast food, eats lots of chocolate?”* | UV radiation, hot drinks, alcohol, spicy food, temperature extremes. *Clue: “red faced after a hot curry and beer”* |
| **Microbial / Infectious** | *Cutibacterium acnes* (overgrowth, not primary cause). *Clue: “responds to benzoyl peroxide/antibiotics”* | *Demodex folliculorum* (increased density). *Bacillus oleronius* (bacteria from mites). *Stem clue: “peripustular mite on biopsy”* |
| **Medication** | Corticosteroids (steroid acne: monomorphic papules), lithium, isoniazid, haloperidol. *Clue: “patient on systemic steroids for asthma”* | Topical corticosteroids (can induce perioral dermatitis rosacea-like). *Clue: “using steroid cream on face”* |
| **Trauma** | Picking, friction (acne mechanica — e.g., helmet straps). *Clue: “acne on forehead in rugby player”* | Not a typical trigger |
| **Systemic Disease** | PCOS, congenital adrenal hyperplasia, hyperandrogenism. *Clue: “acne + hirsutism + acanthosis nigricans”* | Possibly associated with metabolic syndrome, migraine, autoimmune thyroiditis. *Clue: “rosacea patient with hypertension, abdominal obesity”* |

**Most Common Cause**:  
- **Acne:** Multifactorial (genetic + androgens + *C. acnes*).  
- **Rosacea:** Idiopathic with strong environmental/trigger component; Demodex is a contributory factor in papulopustular subtype.

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M: **Rule #1:** Both are **clinical diagnoses**. Biopsy is rarely needed if history and exam are classic. Investigations are reserved for atypical cases or when you suspect underlying disease.
sections.5.1 Diagnostic Criteria (Clinical):
**Acne Vulgaris Grading:**  
- **Leeds Acne Grading System** (simplified): grade 1 (comedones only) → grade 2 (papules/pustules, no nodules) → grade 3 (scattered nodules) → grade 4 (extensive, painful nodules, cysts, sinus tracts).  
  *Pattern recognition:* If you chart lesions on a diagram, the count helps decide if systemic therapy (e.g., isotretinoin) is needed.

**Rosacea Classification (National Rosacea Society, now standard):**  
- **Erythematotelangiectatic (ETR)** – flushing, persistent redness, telangiectasias  
- **Papulopustular (PPR)** – papules and pustules on a red background, no comedones  
- **Phymatous** – thickened skin, especially nose (rhinophyma)  
- **Ocular** – blepharitis, conjunctivitis, keratitis  

*Pattern recognition:* If a patient has flushing + red papules + no comedones + eye irritation → diagnose PPR + ocular rosacea.
sections.5.2 Tests to Consider (When to Do Them):
| **Test** | **What It Shows** | **Why You Order It** | **Interpretation** |
|----------|-------------------|----------------------|-------------------|
| **Serum free androgen index, LH/FSH, DHEAS** | Elevated in female with acne if PCOS/adrenal hyperplasia suspected | Acne + irregular periods + hirsutism | Elevated free testosterone or DHEAS → endocrine referral; FSH/LH ratio >2 suggests PCOS |
| **Thyroid function tests (TSH)** | May be abnormal in refractory rosacea | Rosacea that doesn't respond to standard therapy | Low TSH → hyperthyroid (can mimic flushing) |
| **Skin swab for culture** | Gram-negative rods (*Pseudomonas, Klebsiella, Proteus*) | Sudden worsening; perinasal pustules after long antibiotics | Gram-negative folliculitis → stop current antibiotics; treat with isotretinoin or TMP-SMX |
| **Patch testing** | Delayed hypersensitivity to cosmetics | Rosacea that flares with facial products | Positive allergens → avoidance helps |
| **Skin biopsy** | Histopathology: comedones in acne; perifollicular lymphohistiocytic infiltrate, Demodex, telangiectasia in rosacea | Only if diagnosis uncertain (rare) | Acne shows microcomedones + sebaceous hypertrophy; rosacea shows no comedones, Demodex, granulomatous inflammation (if phymatous) |

**Single Finding That Confirms (or Rules Out): The Comedone Test**

- **One open or closed comedone** → acne, never rosacea. If you see a single blackhead or whitehead, you can confidently rule out rosacea as the *primary* diagnosis.
- **Erythematous flare with telangiectasias on central face + no comedones** → rosacea.

---
sections.6.1 Immediate (First Hour / Day of Diagn:
**Acne:**
- **Topical benzoyl peroxide 2.5–10% gel** once daily (apply to affected areas) – kills *C. acnes*, mild anti-inflammatory.  
  *Why:* Start simple. BP doesn't cause antibiotic resistance. Can be used alone for mild comedonal acne.  
  *Pitfall:* Avoid is less efficacious than creams? No, gel usually better for acne. Warn about bleaching clothes.
- **If pustules present:** Add **topical clindamycin 1%** or **erythromycin 2%** twice daily (limited duration – no longer than 3 months to avoid resistance).

**Rosacea:**
- **Avoid all triggers immediately:** Sun protection (SPF 50+, physical blocker like zinc oxide), stop hot drinks, alcohol, spicy food.
- **Topical metronidazole 0.75% gel or cream** once daily (apply to red/pustular areas).  
  *Why:* Reduces Demodex load and anti-inflammatory. Takes 8-12 weeks.
- **If flushing severe:** Consider **clonidine 50 mcg PO twice daily** (off-label) – reduces sympathetic outflow.  
  *Why:* Only for neurogenic flushing; check blood pressure (risk hypotension).
sections.6.2 Ward / 24–72 Hours (If Hospitalised,:
- Acne/rosacea rarely require admission unless secondary infection (cellulitis) or very severe acne with systemic symptoms (acne fulminans: fever, arthralgias, lytic bone lesions).  
  **If acne fulminans:**  
  - Start **oral prednisolone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day** (to suppress inflammation) then taper.  
  - Then add **oral isotretinoin 0.5–1 mg/kg/day** after 2–4 weeks of steroids.  
  - **Do not start isotretinoin without steroids first** – can worsen.

- **Rosacea with ocular keratitis:** Urgent ophthalmology consult. **Doxycycline 100 mg PO twice daily** x 4–8 weeks reduces tear film bacterial lipases and MMP-9.
sections.6.3 Long-Term Management (Outpatient):
**Acne Algorithm (Stepwise):**

- **Mild (comedones, few papules):**  
  Topical retinoid (e.g., **adapalene 0.1% gel** nocturnal) + benzoyl peroxide as needed.  
  *Why:* Retinoids normalise hyperkeratosis; BP kills *C. acnes*.

- **Moderate (papules/pustules, scattered nodules):**  
  Add **oral antibiotic**: **Lymecycline 408 mg PO once daily** (preferred due to lower photosensitivity) or **doxycycline 100 mg PO once daily** for 12 weeks.  
  *Why:* Anti-inflammatory effect beyond antibacterial (matrix metalloproteinase inhibition).  
  **Always combine with topical retinoid + BP** to prevent resistance.

- **Severe (nodulocystic, scarring, or failed 3 months of antibiotics):**  
  **Oral isotretinoin** 0.5–1 mg/kg/day for 4–6 months (target cumulative dose ~120–150 mg/kg).  
  *Why:* The only agent that targets all four pathogenic steps.  
  **Monitoring:** Monthly LFTs, fasting lipids, pregnancy test (all female patients of childbearing potential MUST use two forms of contraception – teratogenic).  
  *Pitfall:* Do not stop if transaminitis mild (<3x normal); hold if severe or if cheilitis causing functional issues (oral moisturising lip balm mandatory).

**Rosacea Long-Term:**

- **Papulopustular subtype:**  
  - Topical: **ivermectin 1% cream** once daily (24 weeks) – anti-parasitic/cath-mediated effect; or **azelaic acid 15% gel** twice daily (anti-inflammatory, reduces LL-37).  
  - Oral: **oral doxycycline 40–100 mg daily** (sub-antimicrobial dose still has anti-inflammatory effect).  
  - **Low-dose isotretinoin** 10–20 mg daily for refractory papulopustular rosacea.

- **Erythematotelangiectatic subtype:**  
  - Sun protection, trigger avoidance, **brimonidine 0.33% gel** (vasoconstrictor – reduces redness temporarily; can cause rebound).  
  - **IPL / pulsed dye laser** for telangiectasias.

- **Phymatous subtype:**  
  - **CO2 laser resurfacing** or surgical debulking.

- **Ocular rosacea:**  
  - Warm compresses, artificial tears, **loteprednol etabonate 0.2% ophthalmic suspension** (short courses).  
  - Oral doxycycline 40–100 mg daily for severe cases.
sections.**What NOT to Do (Common Pitfalls)**:
- **Don’t use topical steroids on rosacea** – they cause rebound redness, perioral dermatitis, and worsen Demodex.  
- **Don’t prescribe oral antibiotics for acne without topical retinoid** – you create resistant *C. acnes* and lose the only benefit (anti-inflammatory).  
- **Don’t start isotretinoin in acne fulminans without prednisone first.**  
- **Don’t forget pregnancy test before isotretinoin.**  
- **Don’t overuse topical metronidazole alone for acne** – it has no comedolytic effect and will not help comedones.  
- **Don’t ignore ocular symptoms in rosacea** – permanent corneal damage can occur.

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Acne Vulgaris | Rosacea (Papulopustular) | Perioral Dermatitis | Sebaceous Hyperplasia | Folliculitis (Bacterial) |
|---------|---------------|--------------------------|---------------------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| **Age at onset** | Adolescence / young adult | 30–60 years | Young women (20-40) | Middle-aged > elderly | Any age |
| **Distribution** | Face, chest, back (sebaceous regions) | Central face (cheeks, nose, chin; spares perioral) | Perioral (nasolabial folds, chin), spares vermilion border | Face, especially forehead, cheeks | Beard area, scalp, buttocks; often hair-bearing |
| **Lesion types** | Comedones + papules + pustules + nodules/cysts | Papules + pustules on flushed background; telangiectasias; *no comedones* | Small erythematous papules, fine scaling, sometimes pustules | Yellow/pearly dome-shaped papules with central dell (like a donut) | Erythematous follicular papules and pustules; central hair often visible |
| **Flushing / triggers** | Rare | Very common (hot drinks, alcohol, sun) | Not a feature | No | No |
| **Ocular involvement** | No | Yes (blepharitis, dry eyes) | No | No | No |
| **Rhinophyma** | No | Possible (phymatous subtype) | No | No | No |
| **Key distinguishing feature** | **Comedones!** | **Absence of comedones + flushing + telangiectasias** | **Sparing of vermilion border** | **Dell/umbilication in centre of each papule** | **Centered on hair follicle + culture may grow *Staph aureus*** |

**Mnemonic to Differentiate Rosacea vs Perioral Dermatitis vs Acne:**

- **ROSACEA:** Red, Oily? No. Sebaceous excess? No. Acne-like? No comedones. Central. Eyes. Adult.  
- **PERIORAL:** Positive for lip-zone clearance.  
- **ACNE:** Always comedones.

---
sections.Acne Vulgaris:
| **Complication** | **How It Presents** | **Key Signs** | **What to Do** |
|------------------|---------------------|---------------|----------------|
| **Atrophic scarring** | Depressed pitted scars (“ice-pick”, “boxcar”) or rolling scars | Visible after resolution of inflammation; worse if patient picked | Early isotretinoin to prevent new scarring; later laser resurfacing, dermabrasion, fillers |
| **Hypertrophic / keloid scarring** | Raised, firm, sometimes itchy scars on chest/back/face | More common in darker skin types | Intralesional steroids (triamcinolone 10–40 mg/mL), silicone gel |
| **Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation (PIH)** |

rawMarkdown:
# Acne Vulgaris & Rosacea

**One-liner:** Two common chronic inflammatory facial dermatoses: acne (adolescents, closed/open comedones, inflamed nodules) vs rosacea (adults >30, central flushing, telangiectasias, papules/pustules but no comedones). Both disfiguring and treatable — misdiagnosis worsens outcomes.

---

## 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You’ll See

**Classic Acne Vignette:**  
A 17-year-old male presents with a 6-month history of spots on his face, upper chest, and back. He has both blackheads and whiteheads, plus red, tender papules and pustules. Some deeper, larger lumps under the skin are painful. He has tried over-the-counter benzoyl peroxide washes and salicylic acid. He feels embarrassed, avoids social situations, and picks at the lesions. There is no history of flushing or eye discomfort.

- **Teenager** → hormonal surge, sebum overproduction  
- **Face + upper trunk** → distribution of pilosebaceous units (sebaceous glands are largest here)  
- **Mixed lesions: comedones + inflammatory** → classic acne vulgaris; comedones are pathognomonic  
- **Painful deep nodules** → indicates inflammatory papules and cysts (risk of scarring)  
- **Picking** → can lead to secondary infection, hyperpigmentation, scars  
- **No flushing or ocular symptoms** → rules against rosacea  

**Classic Rosacea Vignette:**  
A 45-year-old woman with fair skin presents with persistent redness on her cheeks, nose, and central forehead. She reports episodes of flushing triggered by hot drinks, spicy food, and sun exposure. She has small red bumps and pus-filled spots on the same areas, but no blackheads or whiteheads. She also describes gritty, dry eyes and occasional blurring.

- **Middle-aged, fair-skinned, female** → typical demographics (rosacea women > men, but men more severe if later)  
- **Central face: cheeks, nose, forehead** → erythema and telangiectasias; spares perioral and periocular zones  
- **Flushing + triggers** → vascular dysregulation hallmark  
- **No comedones** → this is the single best differentiating feature from acne  
- **Papules and pustules on erythematous background** → “rose” color  
- **Ocular symptoms: gritty, dry eyes** → ocular rosacea (40-60% of patients, often missed)  

**Atypical Presentations That Can Trick You:**

|**Trickster Variant**|**Presentation**|**Why It’s a Trap**|
|---|---|---|
|Adult-onset acne (20s-30s) | Woman 28 with chin/jawline inflammatory cysts, sometimes with oily skin, mild hirsutism | Might be mistaken for rosacea because of age and facial redness; look for comedones and menstrual flare to confirm acne |
|Gram-negative folliculitis| Patient with long-term oral antibiotics for acne gets sudden worsening of perinasal pustules | Misinterpreted as treatment failure → could actually be iatrogenic (add swab+) |
|Rosacea fulminans (pyoderma faciale)| Sudden explosive pustular eruption on face in young women | Looks severe like cystic acne but no comedones; often confused with acne fulminans (acne fulminans has systemic symptoms) |

---

## 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Acne Vulgaris:** A chronic inflammatory disease of the pilosebaceous unit driven by four interlinked processes: follicular hyperkeratinisation leading to microcomedones, increased sebum production (androgens), colonisation by *Cutibacterium acnes*, and inflammation. It is the most common skin disease worldwide, affecting nearly 85% of adolescents and young adults.

**Rosacea:** A chronic cutaneous disorder of the central face characterised by flushing, persistent erythema, telangiectasias, and inflammatory papules/pustules. It is not a disease of the sebaceous follicle per se — there is no hyperkeratosis or excessive sebum — but rather a neurovascular dysregulation with aberrant innate immune responses. Rosacea almost always spares the perioral and periocular zones and is seen in adults, peaking in the 40-60 age group.

**Why They Matter Clinically:**

- **Frequency:** Acne is ubiquitous — you will see it daily in primary care, paediatrics, dermatology, and even emergency (for things like hydradenitis suppurativa dx). Rosacea affects up to 10% of adults.
- **Psychosocial Impact:** Both cause significant depression, anxiety, social withdrawal, and reduced QoL. Acne scarring is permanent.
- **Common Pitfalls:** Mistaking rosacea for acne leads to inappropriate topical retinoids/antibiotics that worsen flushing; mistaking acne for rosacea can deny a patient isotretinoin when indicated. Ocular rosacea is frequently not asked about, leading to corneal damage.
- **Medical Consequences:** Acne scarring is permanent; rosacea can cause keratitis and vision loss; both can be associated with systemic diseases (acne with PCOS/androgen excess; rosacea with metabolic syndrome? — evidence mixed but plausible).

---

## 3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind The Findings

**Acne — The Story in 4 Acts:**

**Act 1: Follicular hyperkeratosis.**  
Androgens stimulate both sebaceous gland size and activity. Inside the follicle, keratinocytes fail to desquamate normally, causing a plug — the **microcomedone**. This is the precursor of all acne lesions.  
*Why you see comedones:* The follicular opening becomes blocked. If open → blackhead (melanin keratin debris oxidises); if closed → whitehead.

**Act 2: Sebum overproduction.**  
Androgens (especially dihydrotestosterone) drive sebocytes to produce sebum rich in triglycerides and squalene. The follicle becomes distended.  
*Why you see oily skin:* Sebum excretion rate is increased.

**Act 3: *C. acnes* colonisation.**  
This commensal anaerobe thrives in the lipid-rich, oxygen-poor environment of the blocked follicle. It releases lipases, proteases, and hyaluronidases.  
*Why inflammation starts:* *C. acnes* activates toll-like receptor 2 (TLR2) on keratinocytes and macrophages, triggering IL-1, TNF-α, and complement. The follicle wall ruptures.

**Act 4: Inflammation and scarring.**  
Rupture of the follicular contents (keratin, sebum, *C. acnes*) into the dermis triggers a neutrophilic and lymphocytic response. This gives **inflammatory papules, pustules, nodules, and cysts**.  
*Why scars form:* The dermis is damaged. Collagenolytic activity and aberrant healing produce atrophic or hypertrophic scars.

**Rosacea — The Story of an Overreactive Innate System**

**Core abnormality:** An exaggerated innate immune response with neurovascular instability, not primarily a follicular problem.

**Trigger → Flushing:**  
Emotional stress, heat, UV, alcohol, and spicy food cause vasodilation via neuropeptide release (substance P, CGRP) from sensory nerves.  
*Why you see flushing and persistent erythema:* Repeated vasodilation over years leads to telangiectasias (dilated superficial capillaries) and chronic edema from leaky vessels.

**Inflammatory papules and pustules:**  
In rosacea, keratinocytes express high levels of cathelicidin (LL-37) and kallikrein 5. This antimicrobial peptide, when abnormally cleaved, becomes pro-inflammatory. The inflammation is perifollicular, but comedones are *absent* because there is no hyperkeratosis.  
*Why no comedones:* The follicular opening remains patent; the inflammation is in the surrounding dermis, not the follicle neck.

**Demodex mites:** The commensal *Demodex folliculorum* is found in higher densities in rosacea patients. They may act as a trigger by activating TLRs and producing bacterial products (*Bacillus oleronius*).  
*Why you see papules and pustules on a red background:* Mite-induced inflammation adds to the innate immune response.

**Ocular rosacea:** Melbomian gland dysfunction with tear film instability leads to blepharitis, conjunctival hyperaemia, and corneal damage.  
*Why gritty eyes:* Meibum quality changes → dry eye with inflammation.

**Connection to Pattern Recognition:**

- The **absence of comedones** in rosacea is the single most reliable clue to differentiate it from acne — histologically there is no follicular hyperkeratosis.
- The **central face distribution** with sparing of perioral area in rosacea vs. the full-face-plus-trunk of acne reflects different anatomical predilection: rosacea affects the “flush area” (triggered by vascular units), while acne hits sebaceous units anywhere.
- **Flushing** is pathognomonic for rosacea and never part of acne.

---

## 4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins)

| **Category** | **Acne Vulgaris** | **Rosacea** |
|--------------|-------------------|-------------|
| **Idiopathic** | Multifactorial; genetic predisposition (twin studies: 50–80% heritability) | Genetic: familial clustering, HLA antigens; specific genes unknown |
| **Hormonal** | Androgens (adrenarche, puberty, PCOS, anabolic steroids, pregnancy). *Stem clue: “patient with irregular periods, hirsutism, obesity”* | Not primarily hormonal; but perimenopausal women may flare. *Stem clue: “midlife woman with hot flushes and facial redness”* |
| **Environmental / Lifestyle** | Diet: high glycemic index, dairy (skim milk? controversial). *Clue: “works in fast food, eats lots of chocolate?”* | UV radiation, hot drinks, alcohol, spicy food, temperature extremes. *Clue: “red faced after a hot curry and beer”* |
| **Microbial / Infectious** | *Cutibacterium acnes* (overgrowth, not primary cause). *Clue: “responds to benzoyl peroxide/antibiotics”* | *Demodex folliculorum* (increased density). *Bacillus oleronius* (bacteria from mites). *Stem clue: “peripustular mite on biopsy”* |
| **Medication** | Corticosteroids (steroid acne: monomorphic papules), lithium, isoniazid, haloperidol. *Clue: “patient on systemic steroids for asthma”* | Topical corticosteroids (can induce perioral dermatitis rosacea-like). *Clue: “using steroid cream on face”* |
| **Trauma** | Picking, friction (acne mechanica — e.g., helmet straps). *Clue: “acne on forehead in rugby player”* | Not a typical trigger |
| **Systemic Disease** | PCOS, congenital adrenal hyperplasia, hyperandrogenism. *Clue: “acne + hirsutism + acanthosis nigricans”* | Possibly associated with metabolic syndrome, migraine, autoimmune thyroiditis. *Clue: “rosacea patient with hypertension, abdominal obesity”* |

**Most Common Cause**:  
- **Acne:** Multifactorial (genetic + androgens + *C. acnes*).  
- **Rosacea:** Idiopathic with strong environmental/trigger component; Demodex is a contributory factor in papulopustular subtype.

---

## 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Rule #1:** Both are **clinical diagnoses**. Biopsy is rarely needed if history and exam are classic. Investigations are reserved for atypical cases or when you suspect underlying disease.

### 5.1 Diagnostic Criteria (Clinical)

**Acne Vulgaris Grading:**  
- **Leeds Acne Grading System** (simplified): grade 1 (comedones only) → grade 2 (papules/pustules, no nodules) → grade 3 (scattered nodules) → grade 4 (extensive, painful nodules, cysts, sinus tracts).  
  *Pattern recognition:* If you chart lesions on a diagram, the count helps decide if systemic therapy (e.g., isotretinoin) is needed.

**Rosacea Classification (National Rosacea Society, now standard):**  
- **Erythematotelangiectatic (ETR)** – flushing, persistent redness, telangiectasias  
- **Papulopustular (PPR)** – papules and pustules on a red background, no comedones  
- **Phymatous** – thickened skin, especially nose (rhinophyma)  
- **Ocular** – blepharitis, conjunctivitis, keratitis  

*Pattern recognition:* If a patient has flushing + red papules + no comedones + eye irritation → diagnose PPR + ocular rosacea.

### 5.2 Tests to Consider (When to Do Them)

| **Test** | **What It Shows** | **Why You Order It** | **Interpretation** |
|----------|-------------------|----------------------|-------------------|
| **Serum free androgen index, LH/FSH, DHEAS** | Elevated in female with acne if PCOS/adrenal hyperplasia suspected | Acne + irregular periods + hirsutism | Elevated free testosterone or DHEAS → endocrine referral; FSH/LH ratio >2 suggests PCOS |
| **Thyroid function tests (TSH)** | May be abnormal in refractory rosacea | Rosacea that doesn't respond to standard therapy | Low TSH → hyperthyroid (can mimic flushing) |
| **Skin swab for culture** | Gram-negative rods (*Pseudomonas, Klebsiella, Proteus*) | Sudden worsening; perinasal pustules after long antibiotics | Gram-negative folliculitis → stop current antibiotics; treat with isotretinoin or TMP-SMX |
| **Patch testing** | Delayed hypersensitivity to cosmetics | Rosacea that flares with facial products | Positive allergens → avoidance helps |
| **Skin biopsy** | Histopathology: comedones in acne; perifollicular lymphohistiocytic infiltrate, Demodex, telangiectasia in rosacea | Only if diagnosis uncertain (rare) | Acne shows microcomedones + sebaceous hypertrophy; rosacea shows no comedones, Demodex, granulomatous inflammation (if phymatous) |

**Single Finding That Confirms (or Rules Out): The Comedone Test**

- **One open or closed comedone** → acne, never rosacea. If you see a single blackhead or whitehead, you can confidently rule out rosacea as the *primary* diagnosis.
- **Erythematous flare with telangiectasias on central face + no comedones** → rosacea.

---

## 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

### 6.1 Immediate (First Hour / Day of Diagnosis)

**Acne:**
- **Topical benzoyl peroxide 2.5–10% gel** once daily (apply to affected areas) – kills *C. acnes*, mild anti-inflammatory.  
  *Why:* Start simple. BP doesn't cause antibiotic resistance. Can be used alone for mild comedonal acne.  
  *Pitfall:* Avoid is less efficacious than creams? No, gel usually better for acne. Warn about bleaching clothes.
- **If pustules present:** Add **topical clindamycin 1%** or **erythromycin 2%** twice daily (limited duration – no longer than 3 months to avoid resistance).

**Rosacea:**
- **Avoid all triggers immediately:** Sun protection (SPF 50+, physical blocker like zinc oxide), stop hot drinks, alcohol, spicy food.
- **Topical metronidazole 0.75% gel or cream** once daily (apply to red/pustular areas).  
  *Why:* Reduces Demodex load and anti-inflammatory. Takes 8-12 weeks.
- **If flushing severe:** Consider **clonidine 50 mcg PO twice daily** (off-label) – reduces sympathetic outflow.  
  *Why:* Only for neurogenic flushing; check blood pressure (risk hypotension).

### 6.2 Ward / 24–72 Hours (If Hospitalised, Unlikely but Scenario)

- Acne/rosacea rarely require admission unless secondary infection (cellulitis) or very severe acne with systemic symptoms (acne fulminans: fever, arthralgias, lytic bone lesions).  
  **If acne fulminans:**  
  - Start **oral prednisolone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day** (to suppress inflammation) then taper.  
  - Then add **oral isotretinoin 0.5–1 mg/kg/day** after 2–4 weeks of steroids.  
  - **Do not start isotretinoin without steroids first** – can worsen.

- **Rosacea with ocular keratitis:** Urgent ophthalmology consult. **Doxycycline 100 mg PO twice daily** x 4–8 weeks reduces tear film bacterial lipases and MMP-9.

### 6.3 Long-Term Management (Outpatient)

**Acne Algorithm (Stepwise):**

- **Mild (comedones, few papules):**  
  Topical retinoid (e.g., **adapalene 0.1% gel** nocturnal) + benzoyl peroxide as needed.  
  *Why:* Retinoids normalise hyperkeratosis; BP kills *C. acnes*.

- **Moderate (papules/pustules, scattered nodules):**  
  Add **oral antibiotic**: **Lymecycline 408 mg PO once daily** (preferred due to lower photosensitivity) or **doxycycline 100 mg PO once daily** for 12 weeks.  
  *Why:* Anti-inflammatory effect beyond antibacterial (matrix metalloproteinase inhibition).  
  **Always combine with topical retinoid + BP** to prevent resistance.

- **Severe (nodulocystic, scarring, or failed 3 months of antibiotics):**  
  **Oral isotretinoin** 0.5–1 mg/kg/day for 4–6 months (target cumulative dose ~120–150 mg/kg).  
  *Why:* The only agent that targets all four pathogenic steps.  
  **Monitoring:** Monthly LFTs, fasting lipids, pregnancy test (all female patients of childbearing potential MUST use two forms of contraception – teratogenic).  
  *Pitfall:* Do not stop if transaminitis mild (<3x normal); hold if severe or if cheilitis causing functional issues (oral moisturising lip balm mandatory).

**Rosacea Long-Term:**

- **Papulopustular subtype:**  
  - Topical: **ivermectin 1% cream** once daily (24 weeks) – anti-parasitic/cath-mediated effect; or **azelaic acid 15% gel** twice daily (anti-inflammatory, reduces LL-37).  
  - Oral: **oral doxycycline 40–100 mg daily** (sub-antimicrobial dose still has anti-inflammatory effect).  
  - **Low-dose isotretinoin** 10–20 mg daily for refractory papulopustular rosacea.

- **Erythematotelangiectatic subtype:**  
  - Sun protection, trigger avoidance, **brimonidine 0.33% gel** (vasoconstrictor – reduces redness temporarily; can cause rebound).  
  - **IPL / pulsed dye laser** for telangiectasias.

- **Phymatous subtype:**  
  - **CO2 laser resurfacing** or surgical debulking.

- **Ocular rosacea:**  
  - Warm compresses, artificial tears, **loteprednol etabonate 0.2% ophthalmic suspension** (short courses).  
  - Oral doxycycline 40–100 mg daily for severe cases.

### **What NOT to Do (Common Pitfalls)**

- **Don’t use topical steroids on rosacea** – they cause rebound redness, perioral dermatitis, and worsen Demodex.  
- **Don’t prescribe oral antibiotics for acne without topical retinoid** – you create resistant *C. acnes* and lose the only benefit (anti-inflammatory).  
- **Don’t start isotretinoin in acne fulminans without prednisone first.**  
- **Don’t forget pregnancy test before isotretinoin.**  
- **Don’t overuse topical metronidazole alone for acne** – it has no comedolytic effect and will not help comedones.  
- **Don’t ignore ocular symptoms in rosacea** – permanent corneal damage can occur.

---

## 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Acne Vulgaris | Rosacea (Papulopustular) | Perioral Dermatitis | Sebaceous Hyperplasia | Folliculitis (Bacterial) |
|---------|---------------|--------------------------|---------------------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| **Age at onset** | Adolescence / young adult | 30–60 years | Young women (20-40) | Middle-aged > elderly | Any age |
| **Distribution** | Face, chest, back (sebaceous regions) | Central face (cheeks, nose, chin; spares perioral) | Perioral (nasolabial folds, chin), spares vermilion border | Face, especially forehead, cheeks | Beard area, scalp, buttocks; often hair-bearing |
| **Lesion types** | Comedones + papules + pustules + nodules/cysts | Papules + pustules on flushed background; telangiectasias; *no comedones* | Small erythematous papules, fine scaling, sometimes pustules | Yellow/pearly dome-shaped papules with central dell (like a donut) | Erythematous follicular papules and pustules; central hair often visible |
| **Flushing / triggers** | Rare | Very common (hot drinks, alcohol, sun) | Not a feature | No | No |
| **Ocular involvement** | No | Yes (blepharitis, dry eyes) | No | No | No |
| **Rhinophyma** | No | Possible (phymatous subtype) | No | No | No |
| **Key distinguishing feature** | **Comedones!** | **Absence of comedones + flushing + telangiectasias** | **Sparing of vermilion border** | **Dell/umbilication in centre of each papule** | **Centered on hair follicle + culture may grow *Staph aureus*** |

**Mnemonic to Differentiate Rosacea vs Perioral Dermatitis vs Acne:**

- **ROSACEA:** Red, Oily? No. Sebaceous excess? No. Acne-like? No comedones. Central. Eyes. Adult.  
- **PERIORAL:** Positive for lip-zone clearance.  
- **ACNE:** Always comedones.

---

## 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

### Acne Vulgaris

| **Complication** | **How It Presents** | **Key Signs** | **What to Do** |
|------------------|---------------------|---------------|----------------|
| **Atrophic scarring** | Depressed pitted scars (“ice-pick”, “boxcar”) or rolling scars | Visible after resolution of inflammation; worse if patient picked | Early isotretinoin to prevent new scarring; later laser resurfacing, dermabrasion, fillers |
| **Hypertrophic / keloid scarring** | Raised, firm, sometimes itchy scars on chest/back/face | More common in darker skin types | Intralesional steroids (triamcinolone 10–40 mg/mL), silicone gel |
| **Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation (PIH)** |
